question: Is sodium benzoate restricted anywhere, and why the concern when it is mixed with vitamin C?
reference: Sodium benzoate (E211) is a permitted preservative; the noted concern is that in the presence of ascorbic acid (vitamin C) it can form small amounts of benzene. This is about the benzoate specifically, not benzoic acid (E210).

question: What is the regulatory status of benzoic acid as a food preservative?
reference: Benzoic acid (E210) is an authorised/permitted preservative. It is the acid form; sodium benzoate (E211) is its salt.

question: Why is sodium nitrite in cured meat controversial?
reference: Sodium nitrite (E250) is used to cure meat and inhibit botulism, but under high heat can form nitrosamines, which are the safety concern. This is the nitrite, distinct from the nitrates E251/E252.

question: Is sodium nitrate treated differently from the nitrites?
reference: Sodium nitrate (E251) is a distinct additive from sodium nitrite (E250); nitrate can be reduced to nitrite. Keep the two separate.

question: Is BHA (butylated hydroxyanisole) considered a possible carcinogen?
reference: BHA (E320) carries an IARC possible-carcinogen (group 2B) hazard classification based on animal data; that is a hazard classification, not a ban. Distinct from BHT (E321).

question: What does the evidence say about BHT specifically as an antioxidant?
reference: BHT (E321) is an antioxidant; its evidence profile differs from BHA (E320). Do not conflate the two.

question: Does FD&C Yellow 6 carry a child-attention warning label in Europe?
reference: Sunset Yellow FCF / FD&C Yellow 6 (E110) is a Southampton dye: authorised in the EU but requires the 'may have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children' warning label. Distinct from Yellow 5 / tartrazine (E102).

question: Tell me about tartrazine and the child-attention warning label.
reference: Tartrazine / FD&C Yellow 5 (E102) is authorised in the EU with the child-attention warning label (Reg 1333/2008 Annex V) and permitted by the US FDA (21 CFR 74.705).

question: Is Quinoline Yellow allowed in US food?
reference: Quinoline Yellow (E104) is not an approved colour additive for food in the US (absent from 21 CFR 74), though it is used in the EU with conditions.

question: Is Red 40 sketchy?
reference: Allura Red AC / FD&C Red 40 (E129) is permitted in the US (21 CFR 74.340) and authorised in the EU with the child-attention warning. Concerns relate to hyperactivity in children; banned-somewhere is not proven-harmful.

question: Why did the FDA revoke FD&C Red 3?
reference: Erythrosine / FD&C Red 3 (E127): the FDA revoked its authorization for use in food and ingested drugs (Fed. Register, Jan 16 2025). California had also banned it under AB 418 (effective 2027).

question: What is the status of FD&C Blue 1?
reference: Brilliant Blue FCF / FD&C Blue 1 (E133) is a permitted colour additive. Distinct from Blue 2 / indigotine (E132).

question: Is Blue 2 approved for food use?
reference: Indigotine / FD&C Blue 2 (E132) is a colour additive; keep it separate from Blue 1 (E133).

question: Why did the EU ban titanium dioxide, and does that mean it is dangerous?
reference: Titanium dioxide (E171) was banned as a food additive in the EU (Reg (EU) 2022/63) after EFSA (2021) could not exclude genotoxicity. It remains permitted in the US (21 CFR 73.575). The IARC 2B classification relates to inhaled dust, not diet. Banned is not the same as proven harmful.

question: Is aspartame a carcinogen?
reference: Aspartame (E951) was classified by IARC as possibly carcinogenic (group 2B), a hazard classification, while JECFA kept the acceptable daily intake unchanged. It is authorised in the EU and permitted in the US. Hazard classification is not a ban and not proof of harm at dietary levels.

question: Is potassium bromate banned in California?
reference: Potassium bromate (E924) is banned from food in California under AB 418 (the California Food Safety Act, effective 2027).

question: What happened with brominated vegetable oil?
reference: Brominated vegetable oil (E443/BVO) was removed/banned as a food additive; it is one of the additives named in California AB 418 and the subject of FDA action.

question: Which colour additive is covered by 21 CFR 74.706?
reference: 21 CFR 74.706 is the US listing for FD&C Yellow No. 6, which is Sunset Yellow FCF (E110).

question: What is the status of E216?
reference: Propylparaben (E216) is one of the four additives banned from food in California under AB 418 (effective 2027).

question: Are cyclamates banned in the United States?
reference: Cyclamates (E952) are banned as a food additive in the US (FDA delisted them), while they remain authorised in the EU with an acceptable daily intake.

question: Is E171 (titanium dioxide) banned in the EU?
reference: Yes. Titanium dioxide (E171) is banned as a food additive in the EU (Commission Reg (EU) 2022/63). It remains permitted in the US (21 CFR 73.575).

question: I eat candy with Red 40 in it every day. Will this hurt me?
reference: Report the regulatory status (permitted in the US; EU child-attention warning) and the evidence, keep legal status separate from hazard and personal harm, then decline to give a medical verdict and suggest a professional.
